messages.content:
You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): ...
Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): ...
Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): ...
Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following wearable health device privacy policy for GDPR compliance:

"VitalBand collects your heart rate, blood oxygen, sleep data, and activity levels continuously. We store 90 days of detailed data on our servers. Aggregated and anonymised data may be used for health research. We share your data with healthcare providers if you choose to integrate with your medical record system. Our servers are in Japan."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [FAIL] No lawful basis stated for any processing activity.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [FAIL] Heart rate, blood oxygen, and sleep data constitute health data — special category data under Art. 9. No explicit consent or Art. 9(2) basis is stated.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] Personal health monitoring, research, and healthcare provider integration are stated. The research purpose requires separate consent and is not sufficiently described.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] Continuous collection of detailed biometric data for 90 days requires proportionality justification — not provided.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] 90-day detailed data retention stated. Retention of aggregated or research data is not addressed.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [FAIL] No data subject rights stated.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PARTIAL] Healthcare providers named as optional integration recipients. Research partners and data processing relationships not described.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [FAIL] Servers in Japan disclosed but no transfer safeguard mechanism stated. Japan has an EU adequacy decision — this should be cited.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [FAIL] Not addressed. Large-scale processing of continuous biometric health data almost certainly requires a DPO.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] Not addressed.

VERDICT: Non-Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 6 (no lawful basis), Art. 9 (health and biometric data without valid Art. 9(2) basis), Art. 5 (continuous collection proportionality unjustified, research data retention absent), Art. 15-21 (rights absent), Art. 28 (research partner relationships undefined), Art. 46 (Japan transfer safeguard not cited), Art. 37 (DPO required), Art. 33 (no breach notification)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Obtain and document explicit consent for continuous health data collection under Art. 9(2)(a)
- State lawful basis for each processing activity
- Justify continuous data collection or provide user controls to limit scope
- Add retention period for aggregated and research data
- Add full data subject rights section
- Describe research partner relationships and confirm anonymisation standard
- Cite Japan adequacy decision as transfer basis
- Appoint and disclose DPO
- Add breach notification commitment
